item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: RIP imposter Steve Jobs. Wonder if the real one ever had cancer or was as much as a alleged douche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I was a little intrigued by the mysterious animosity between the two entities, but the writing got totally lost in the whole character-with-powers scenario, and I pretty much hated this one. The interview in particular was bad because it explained way too much of the mystery away.

Maybe it's a bias against "entrepreneurs" and in favor of Buddhism in general, but the fact that the Steve Jobs that died was real tells me the one they call SCP-1447-2 is lying. Then again, maybe SCP-1447-2 is a tulpa created by the collective delusions of the cult of personality that grew up around Apple's resurgence, in which case there is not enough evidence to cast doubt. The idea that Steve Jobs is not only alive but has attained magic buddhist superpowers from Magical Orientals seems like a childish fantasy.

Anyway, I was underwhelmed by "murderspeed anti-buddha." If you really wanted it to be a dyed-in-the-robes villain, you should have looked to "wrathful deities," which are often misinterpreted at some demonic buddhist shit by westerners. That would have worked well into the whole misbegotten tulpa theme. Instead we just got the old "faceless dude" thing going on. Seriously, even a mean-faced monk thrashing about would have been better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1447-1 is capable of moving at a ludicrous speed, to the extent that even the Foundation would have severe difficulty capturing the thing during a containment breach. It could easily slaughter thousands of people before contact with the anomaly is reestablished. In addition, its primary goal is the murder of a human being, obviously not as bad as genocide or the like, but bad, and it is willing to kill anyone that gets in its way. Furthermore, it is clearly capable of maintaining its existence without 1447-2, as even 1447-2 concentrating on /exterminating/ the entity failed. The Foundation has no choice but to assume, given its already established and considerably hostile nature, that it will not become harmless after killing 1447-2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all honesty I don't see how this is keter. Worst possible scenario: It breaks containment, kills some agents and mabye an MTF sent to recapture it. It goes to wherever 1447-2 is and kills him. Yes, I do see that there is a chance that once the tulpa kills 1447-2 it will become unstoppable but, so what? So far it hasn't actively tried to hurt anyone except 1447-2. Why would it change?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this one. I feel like the fact that -2 refused to continue the interview was sort of just thrown in there to add mystery and didn't flow well with the rest of the article, but that might just be me. +1, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why, yes, as captain of the Forum Criticism Team, I regularly read the forums.

Shocking, isn't it? :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I linked this in the forums six hours ago at time of writing. Aelanna added the new image template six hours ago at time of writing.

That's suspicious.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I caught this one too lol
Didn't take long AT ALL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcannoned.

Dammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but the wierd Steve jobs refrerence didn't really do it for me
upvoted still

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a hostile, self-sustaining spiritual pattern. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I figured there was some reasoning like that behind it, but it still bothered me. Thanks for the clarification. Don't feel like you have you change it for my sake, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi - thanks! Re the pronouns I was attempting to differentiate between -1 (the entity), which is referred to as 'it' throughout, and -2, as feedback showed people were getting confused between the two. Looking through other SCPs, where -2 is an individual affected or targetted by the skip (the later case being most important here) they are referred to as he/she/they as appropriate; will change if people think that's wrong though. The interview was supposed to indicate that they don't believe -2 is who he claims to be and are generally taking a hostile tone, though the interviewer does try and take a more conciliatory line later by using his name: I'll take another look at it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a minor nit-pick, but I recognize it as a more of a pet peeve of mine, so feel free to disregard it.
SCP-1447-2 is referred to as 'he' throughout the Procedures and Description, where the Foundation would probably use 'it'. This is also somewhat inconsistent with the interview, where the doctor calls him SCP-1447-2, which is actually the problem I have with this. It usually annoys me when the Foundation personnel refer to the interviewee as the designated number in the interviews. I know it's Foundation protocol to use nothing but SCP-whatever, but I think during the interviews they would at least try to establish a common language with the interviewed subject. I'd feel better about it if the designated numbers were in brackets, to indicate that the actual names have been replaced from the interview files with the numbers for the Foundation's purposes. I'm not really sure though what is the correct way to do that. To me it feels the same as if a police interrogation went like this; Cop: "So, 1234-1, during Incident-1234-A, you forced entry to 1234 with 1234-2 and your fingerprints were also found on 1234-3. Now, where is 1234-4?" Suspect: "What?"

The doctor in this case even eventually goes to calling him by his actual name, which to me feels much more natural, but again inconsistent.

But, as I said, this is more of a problem of mine and others may disagree, some may even prove me wrong.
Anyway, I liked this, the mysterious doppelganger in particular, and have voted accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would my tulpae think about this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so bizarre and fun to read that I can't help but love it. I will admit that I thought of Able for the first couple paragraphs, but that doesn't deter from it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that makes it even better. I'm upvoting it just for that. Though as a way to take back Apple founding NeXT and then Pixar seems a mite convoluted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if killing him would only transfer it to attacking the monks, he wouldn't want to chance that the foundation wouldn't leap at the opportunity to transfer it to being some other government's problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, it's Steve Jobs? I thought the article had said he was Asian. Weird.

Anyway, this is really great: the concept, the background, the effect, the containment, the fact that the guy has a doppelganger for some reason. There's some weirdness about the punctuation, but it was not enough to keep me from upvoting.

Also, "murderspeed anti-buddha" in the comments above made me laugh so hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. For some reason, I don't mind that it's Steve Jobs that "made" this thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely like it. The fact that it was Steve Jobs went right over my head somehow so while it did seem a little odd, it wasn't jarring for him to be in there.

But where have I heard of tulpas before? I could've sworn there was another tulpa SCP, but searching for it doesn't turn up anything. Was it deleted or am I thinking of something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing major, don't worry.
The "US citizen" thing was distracting because you hear that line all the time in TV Shows etc - "I'm an american citizen, I know my rights!". I like what you're going for, that he's a bit of a douchebag, but it's the worng kind of douchebag right now if you catch my drift.
As for the high calibre gun-fire thing, there are a few popular ones that come to mind that feature that in a similar way to your SCP - nothing necessary to change, it was more an offhand remark than anything else.
If you're interested, here's a few that came to mind off the top of my head - SCP-096, SCP-076, SCP-682 and SCP-973, (though I'm sure there are more)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "Think Different" reference is a clever one, but not one I actually intended :) I had a number of figures I considered using as models including a 'roll and roll musician of some note' (ultimately rejected as while 'George (or John) is dead' would have been an interesting twist, I didn't want him and the tulpa to have been in containment that long). They were simply well-known figures who had travelled to India or Tibet seeking spiritual experiences. Hopefully apart from the number of blanks it's not absolutely tied to a real historical figure - I dialled back specific references to what happened in 1985 in feedback.

Thanks on the idea :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks: didn't realised just how much I'd overused 'proved'/'proven'. Revised most of these instances and changed the remaining ones to 'proven'.

Re ████ and the murderspeed anti-buddha, I'll have a think about the US citizen bit; I want to convey a certain amount of ingratitude from -2 (he still thinks he can meditate it away and isn't happy about the Foundation's "controlled infliction of pain" tests to see how the tulpa reacts if its creator is roughed up). Hadn't actually seen any other SCPs with the 'corralled by gunfire' thing - thought I was doing something new :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was more a smartass remark than an actual observation. You'd have to ask the author as to why Steve Jobs is in this. Myself, I'm not sure why he needs to be there but it didn't really get in the way of my enjoyment of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why take such a cool and well executed idea and then blow it on a joke :<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember the old "Think Different" ad campaign for Apple? I would imagine creating a homicidal imaginary Buddha-monster with your mind would be a great example of "thinking different."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea alot, but the Steve Jobs thing is just killing it for me. Why does it gotta be Steve Jobs that creates the rogue thought monster?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. I could imagine him shitting his pants at the end of the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only a few minor things rustling my jimmies just now, you use "proved" where proven should be used for a smoother read. I see you used proven elsewhere, were you trying to avoid repetition of proven?
The "US citizen" bit at the end I feel is a little unecessary, it's kind of an unreasonable response to being in the situation where you've created an incredibly fast anti-buddah that is now trying to murder your face.
Lastly, and this is just a general observation about SCPs, but does anybody else feel like this whole "can be slowed down with high-calibre fire but not stopped" gig is getting a little old? I'm totally cool with it being in this SCP, but a few more and it might become a bit stale..
That's my two cents, overall I liked it quite alot - good job man!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember seeing this on the forums and loving it there. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm - although not strictly a contradiction it does beg the question why knowing the monks' opinion he wouldn't just repeat it when asked outright whether his death would destroy 1447-1. I've changed it to 'They were afraid the tulpa would kill them too', which hopefully resolves the issue. If it's still a problem I'll simply change it to 'They were afraid of it'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I knew this story! My Dad told it to me as a way of warding me off, aha, supernatural experimentation…

Anyway, I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is interesting, if not a bit convoluted in certain places.

Also:

Yes. They were afraid once I was dead the tulpa would kill them too. They told me about ██████ .

Dr. R: Will SCP-1447-1 cease to exist if you die? Answer the question.

SCP-1447-2: I refuse to answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's solid but its played too straight and doesn't really do it for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the detail. Reminds me a lot of the tulpa which Alexandra David-Néel claimed to have created on her travels - a fat, jovial monk that became thinner and troublesome.

It's mentioned on the Wikipedia page for Tulpa:

The tulpa concept was brought to the West in the 19th century by Alexandra David-Néel, who claimed to have created a tulpa in the image of a jolly, Friar Tuck-like monk which later developed a life of its own and had to be destroyed.[13] There is a teaching story inherent in Néel's experience as it is evocative of the English rendering of the famous instruction of Zen Master Lin Chi: "If you meet the Buddha on the road, kill him."

So I'm guessing that was the inspiration for this. Not so keen on the Steve Jobs angle in itself (seems jarring), but the interview is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd for many reasons. (Note that the black-boxed person who hired the impersonator has the same number of letters as 'Wozniak'.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it, I will never be able to unthink this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Steve Jobs manifests a thought form and it goes rogue? Brill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nice and solid, and I love your reimagining of Buddhist folklore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview really sold me on this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in the forums, like it here. Upvoted.
